questions.question: What is the range of human hearing in Hz?
questions.explanation: The range of human hearing extends from approximately 20 Hz to 13,000 Hz.

questions.question: What is the speed of sound in air at room temperature?
questions.explanation: The speed of sound in air at room temperature is approximately 343 meters per second.

questions.question: What is the term for the process by which a sound wave is propagated through a medium?
questions.explanation: Sound propagation refers to the process by which a sound wave is transmitted through a medium.

questions.question: What is the unit of measurement for frequency?
questions.explanation: Frequency is measured in Hertz (Hz), which represents the number of oscillations or cycles per second.

questions.question: What is the term for a sound wave with a frequency above 20,000 Hz?
questions.explanation: Ultrasound refers to sound waves with frequencies above 20,000 Hz.

questions.question: What is the term for a sound wave with a frequency below 20 Hz?
questions.explanation: Infrasound refers to sound waves with frequencies below 20 Hz.

questions.question: What is the phenomenon by which a sound wave causes an object to vibrate?
questions.explanation: Sound-induced vibration refers to the phenomenon where a sound wave causes an object to vibrate.

questions.question: What is the term for the measurement of the loudness or intensity of a sound?
questions.explanation: Decibel (dB) is a unit of measurement for the loudness or intensity of a sound.

questions.question: What is the term for a device used to measure the frequency of a sound wave?
questions.explanation: A frequency meter is a device used to measure the frequency of a sound wave.

questions.question: What is the term for the study of sound waves and their properties?
questions.explanation: Acoustics refers to the study of sound waves and their properties.

notes.title: Sound Wave Properties
notes.content: A sound wave is a type of mechanical wave that propagates through a medium, such as air or water. Sound waves have both amplitude and frequency.

notes.title: Types of Sound Waves
notes.content: There are two main types of sound waves: longitudinal and transverse. Longitudinal sound waves propagate in the same direction as the particles of the medium, while transverse sound waves propagate perpendicular to the particles.

notes.title: Sound Propagation
notes.content: Sound propagation refers to the process by which a sound wave is transmitted through a medium. The speed of sound in air at room temperature is approximately 343 meters per second.

notes.title: Frequency and Wavelength
notes.content: The frequency of a sound wave is the number of oscillations or cycles per second, while the wavelength is the distance between two consecutive peaks or troughs.

notes.title: Sound Induced Vibration
notes.content: Sound-induced vibration refers to the phenomenon where a sound wave causes an object to vibrate. This can occur when a sound wave with sufficient intensity strikes an object.

notes.title: Decibel Scale
notes.content: The decibel (dB) is a unit of measurement for the loudness or intensity of a sound. The decibel scale ranges from 0 dB, which represents the threshold of hearing, to over 120 dB, which can cause damage to the human ear.

notes.title: Acoustic Impedance
notes.content: Acoustic impedance is a measure of the opposition to sound wave propagation in a medium. It depends on the density and elasticity of the medium, as well as the frequency of the sound wave.

notes.title: Sound Reflection
notes.content: Sound reflection occurs when a sound wave bounces off an object or surface. This can cause echoes, which are repeated sounds that occur after the original sound has stopped.

notes.title: Sound Absorption
notes.content: Sound absorption refers to the process by which a material reduces the intensity of sound waves. Sound-absorbing materials can be used to reduce echo and improve acoustics in rooms.

notes.title: Acoustic Materials
notes.content: Acoustic materials are designed to manipulate sound waves, either by absorbing or reflecting them. Examples of acoustic materials include fiberglass, mineral wool, and acoustic panels.

facts.fact: The human ear can detect sounds with frequencies between 20 Hz and 13000 Hz.
Sound waves travel at approximately 343 meters per second in air at room temperature.
The decibel (dB) is a unit of measurement for the loudness or intensity of a sound, ranging from 0 dB to over 120 dB.
Acoustic impedance depends on the density and elasticity of the medium, as well as the frequency of the sound wave.
Sound reflection occurs when a sound wave bounces off an object or surface, causing echoes to occur after the original sound has stopped.
Sound absorption refers to the process by which a material reduces the intensity of sound waves, used to reduce echo and improve acoustics in rooms.
Acoustic materials are designed to manipulate sound waves, either by absorbing or reflecting them, such as fiberglass, mineral wool, and acoustic panels.
The Eiffel Tower expands in the summer due to thermal expansion, growing up to 15 cm (6 inches) taller.
Magnets can create levitation using magnetic repulsion, making objects float without touching them.
Sound waves can cause an object to vibrate, a phenomenon known as sound-induced vibration.
